literal: greed, covetousness; from √lubh 'to desire eagerly, to be greedy, to covet', hence 'the state of grasping after gain'
invariant_definition: *Lobha* is greed — the grasping desire for gain and the disinclination to part with what one has. It belongs to the standard lists of mind-defiling forces: in the *Gītā* it stands with *kāma* (desire) and *krodha* (anger) as one of the three 'gates of hell' destructive of the self (16.21), and in the later *ari-ṣaḍ-varga* ('six enemies') it is counted with *kāma, krodha, moha, mada,* and *mātsarya*. Greed is closely tied to *kāma*: where *kāma* is the desire for an object not yet possessed, *lobha* is characteristically the craving to acquire more and to retain, the unwillingness to give. In Buddhist analysis *lobha* (Pali *lobha*) is one of the three unwholesome roots (*akusala-mūla*) alongside *dosa* (hatred) and *moha* (delusion), the root from which all grasping and clinging spring. Across the traditions *lobha* is treated not as a neutral appetite but as a defect (*doṣa*) that distorts judgment, drives unjust action, and binds one to *saṃsāra*; its remedy is *vairāgya* (dispassion) and, in the ethical literature, the cultivation of *dāna* (giving) and contentment.

school_framing.shared_core: *Lobha* names **greed — the grasping desire for gain and the reluctance to give**, listed across traditions among the chief defilements of mind. It is widely shared as a moral-psychological category; schools differ mainly in the taxonomy they place it in.
school_framing.register_axes_note: Largely **shared** as an affliction-term. The *Gītā* groups it with *kāma* and *krodha* as a gate of self-ruin; the smṛti and nīti literature place it in the *ṣaḍ-varga* of inner enemies; Buddhism makes it one of the three unwholesome roots. Same vice, differently catalogued.

per_school.definition: *Lobha* is named at 16.21 with *kāma* and *krodha* as the threefold 'gate of hell' (*trividhaṃ narakasya dvāram*) that ruins the self, and which the seeker is therefore to abandon. It is a mark of the *āsurī sampad* (the demonic endowment) catalogued in chapter 16, and an expression of *rajas*; like the other gates it issues from *kāma* and clouds discernment, so that release from it belongs to the steadying of the *buddhi* and the dispassion of the *sthitaprajña*.
primary_loci: Bhagavad-Gītā 16.21 (kāmaḥ krodhas tathā lobhaḥ; the triple gate)
Bhagavad-Gītā 14.17 (lobha as a fruit of rajas)
per_school.register_tag: M+S; mumukṣu (one of the three gates of hell)

per_school.definition: *Lobha* is one of the three unwholesome roots (*akusala-mūla*) — greed, with hatred (*dosa*) and delusion (*moha*) — from which unwholesome action springs. As craving and clinging in their acquisitive aspect it feeds *taṇhā* and so the arising of suffering; its counterpart wholesome root is *alobha* (non-greed, generosity). The path's cultivation of generosity and dispassion is the direct undoing of *lobha*.
primary_loci: the three akusala-mūla (lobha, dosa, moha) in the suttas and Abhidhamma

textual_history.summary: *Lobha* ('greed') is essentially post-Vedic as a doctrinal vice. The root √lubh ('to desire eagerly, to entice') is Vedic but rare and is attested chiefly in its causative — *pratilobhayantī*, 'enticing, luring away' — not as the noun *lobha* naming a fault of the self. The earliest layer has no developed category of covetousness among the inner enemies. *Lobha* crystallises in the epic-and-Gītā ethical literature: the *Bhagavad-Gītā* names it with *kāma* and *krodha* as one of the three 'gates of hell' (16.21) and as a fruit of *rajas* (14.17), and the smṛti/nīti tradition fixes it in the *ari-ṣaḍ-varga* ('six inner enemies'). The Buddhist *lobha* — one of the three unwholesome roots (*lobha–dosa–moha*) — is the parallel systematisation.

stages.sanskrit: amīṣāṁ cittam pratilobhayantī gṛhāṇāṅgāny apve parehi
stages.rendering: Luring away (pratilobhayantī) the mind of those [foes], O Apvā [demon of panic], seize their limbs and depart.
stages.note: The root √lubh is genuinely Vedic but sparse, and here in its causative sense 'to entice / lure away' — Apvā, the personified terror, 'enticing the mind' of enemies in a battle-charm. This is not yet *lobha* the moral defect: greed as a named inner enemy of the self is post-Vedic. We mark the term's earliest *real* doctrinal attestation as the Gītā (below) rather than read covetousness back into this charm.

stages.sanskrit: tri-vidhaṃ narakasyedaṃ dvāraṃ nāśanam ātmanaḥ | kāmaḥ krodhas tathā lobhas tasmād etat trayaṃ tyajet
stages.rendering: This is the threefold gate of hell, destructive of the self — desire, anger, and greed; therefore let one abandon these three.
stages.note: The Gītā fixes *lobha* in its classic triad with *kāma* and *krodha*, the 'gates of hell', and marks it part of the *āsurī sampad* (the demonic endowment of ch. 16) and a fruit of *rajas* (14.17, *lobhaḥ pravṛttir ārambhaḥ*). This is the seed of the later *ṣaḍ-varga*. Note the structural echo of the Buddhist three unwholesome roots — the same acquisitive/aversive/deluded triad, differently catalogued.

translator_note: 'Greed' or 'covetousness' is accurate; avoid the bland 'desire', which is better reserved for *kāma*. The distinction worth preserving is that *lobha* leans toward acquisition and retention — wanting more, unwillingness to give — whereas *kāma* is desire for an object as such. In Buddhist contexts keep it paired with its catalog (*akusala-mūla*) so the systematic force is not lost.
